mappings.rationaleAI: The nervous system receives sensory information, processes it in the brain and spinal cord, and initiates behavioral responses (C.3), including the autonomic division governing involuntary reactions.
The brain as the central organ for sensory integration, cognition, motor coordination, and behavioral regulation (C.3), processing information and initiating appropriate responses.
The variety of animal behaviour across major clades (A.1) and diverse conceptions of animal behaviour broadly (A).
Phyletic patterns in the evolution of learning (D.2), including non-associative, associative, and complex forms of learning shaped by genetics and experience.
Genetic transmission as evidence for the genetic determination of behaviour (D.1).
Genetic variation and heredity providing evidence for genetic determination of behaviour (D.1), including population genetics approaches.
The full range of human behavioral responses as part of the variety of animal behaviour (A.1) and the influence of experience on behaviour in the evolution of learning (D.2).
Zoology as the scientific discipline covering animal behaviour, ethology, and comparative anatomy, broadly relevant to diverse conceptions of animal behaviour (A).
Botany relevant to plant movements including tropic and nastic movements as stereotyped behavioral responses (B.1).
Insects as the most diverse animal group exhibiting varied behaviour (A.1), with fixed action patterns in metamorphosis (B.2) and photoperiodism affecting life cycles (B.3).

Birds exhibit complex social behavior and cultural transmission (A.1), fixed action patterns including migration and nesting (B.2), and the influence of experience on behaviour through song learning (D.2).
Sex hormones influencing behaviour (C.2), including sexual dimorphism arising from differential selective pressures and sexual selection.
Biological aspects including the sexual response cycle and hormonal influences on sexual behaviour (C.2).
Biological determinants including prenatal hormone exposure as hormonal influences on behaviour (C.2) and genetic factors as evidence for genetic determination of behaviour (D.1).
Hormonal and neurological influences on gender-related behaviour patterns (C.2).

Addiction driven by neurobiological changes in the mesocorticolimbic dopamine-glutamate reward pathway illustrates the interaction of endocrine and nervous systems (C.1) and the nervous system's role in behaviour (C.3).
Memory encoding and retrieval as nervous system functions processing information (C.3) and the influence of experience on behaviour through learning and memory formation (D.2).
Emotions as neurophysiologically grounded states involving interaction of endocrine and nervous systems (C.1) and neural substrates including prefrontal cortex in behavioral regulation (C.3).
Animal communication through verbal and non-verbal channels as a component of animal behaviour (A.3).
Cat behaviour including crepuscular hunting, ambush predation, and pheromone-based communication as examples of the variety of animal behaviour (A.1) and fixed action patterns (B.2).
Dog behaviour including enhanced human social cue interpretation as an example of animal behaviour variety (A.1) and the influence of domestication experience on behaviour evolution (D.2).
